reasoning: Without the specific Polymarket URL or a web search tool, I estimate the probability based on the base rate of the event. GISTEMP releases monthly global temperature anomalies. In recent years, anomalies have been consistently above 1.0°C. 2023 was a very hot El Niño year (often exceeding 1.2-1.3°C), and 2024 was also warm. By late 2025, a shift to a La Niña or neutral phase could bring the global temperature anomaly down relative to the 2023-2024 peak. A value of 1.10°C to 1.14°C is a single 0.05°C-wide bin. Given that typical prediction markets for monthly temperature anomalies comprise several such contiguous bins (often 10 to 15) to cover the likely range (e.g. 1.05 to 1.50+), the baseline probability of landing in any single 0.05°C bin is fairly low. Assuming a roughly normal distribution with standard deviation around 0.1°C to 0.15°C, a moderately central bin has a maximum probability of roughly 10-15%, while a tail bin has a low single-digit chance. I am reasonably placing the probability at approximately 8%.
